answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence:
CATEGORY | SCENARIO 1: ORDERLY | SCENARIO 2: DISORDERLY | SCENARIO 3: HOT HOUSE
Scenario | Net Zero 2050 | Delayed Transition | Current Policies
Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) - Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSP) | SSP1–1.9 | SSP 1–2.6 | SSP 3–7.0
New Zealand Climate Change Commission (CCC) scenarios | Tailwinds | Headwinds | Current policy reference
answers.notes: The three scenarios are also aligned with NGFS categories (Orderly, Disorderly, Hot House) and NZ CS mandated scenarios. The IPCC SSP and CCC scenario equivalents are explicitly listed in the table.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Scenario 1 (Orderly – Net Zero 2050): 1.6°C in 2050 and 1.4°C by 2100 (temperature overshoot then decline). Scenario 2 (Disorderly – Delayed Transition): 1.7°C in 2050 and 1.67°C by 2100. Scenario 3 (Hot House – Current Policies): 2°C by 2050 and more than 3°C by 2100.
answers.evidence: An ambitious and coordinated transition to a low-emissions, climate-resilient future. Stringent climate policies, innovation, ambitious investment, and medium-to-high deployment of carbon removal solutions limit global warming to 1.6°C in 2050 and 1.4°C by 2100. Ambitious action is delayed to 2030, followed by sudden and uncoordinated economic transformation. Extensive, stringent and punitive government intervention, but late government intervention, in combination with some deployment of carbon removal solutions, limits global warming to 1.7°C in 2050 and 1.67°C by 2100. Current emissions reduction policies are implemented. Current socio economic trends continue, resulting in 2°C global warming by 2050 and more than 3°C by 2100.
answers.location_hint: Page 12, Climate Scenarios table – Summary row
answers.notes: The Orderly scenario shows temperature overshoot (1.6°C in 2050 declining to 1.4°C by 2100), consistent with carbon removal solutions. The Hot House scenario exceeds 3°C by 2100.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple providers are explicitly named: NGFS (Network for Greening the Financial System), IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change), and CCC (New Zealand Climate Change Commission). The scenarios were also developed in collaboration with KPMG New Zealand and the NZ retail sector, documented in "The Futures of Retail" report.
answers.evidence: We used publicly available Scenarios, including the Network for Greening Financial System (NGFS), Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), and the New Zealand Climate Change Commission (CCC) Scenarios. [...] In 2023, The Warehouse Group engaged with other New Zealand retailers that are climate reporting entities and KPMG New Zealand to develop shared scenarios for the New Zealand retail sector ("Retail Sector Scenarios"). These scenarios are detailed in a published report entitled "The Futures of Retail".
answers.notes: The scenarios draw on NGFS, IPCC, and CCC as source frameworks, and were co-developed with KPMG NZ and NZ retail peers into the "Retail Sector Scenarios".

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) - Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSP) | SSP1–1.9 | SSP 1–2.6 | SSP 3–7.0
answers.notes: Only SSP identifiers are listed; no RCP identifiers appear anywhere in the document. There is no mixing of SSP and RCP scenario families.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: The Warehouse Group has downscaled the Retail Sector Scenarios and conducted qualitative analysis to develop its climate scenarios and help identify climate-related risks and opportunities over the short, medium and long term. [...] These scenarios are consistent with the retail sector scenarios (but downscaled for TWG's own business), and therefore support comparability with the disclosures of other retailers.
answers.location_hint: Page 10 (Strategy section) and Page 14 (Rationale for these scenarios)
answers.notes: The document explicitly uses the word "downscaled" to describe how the retail sector scenarios were adapted for TWG's own business context. The process involved internal workshops to apply the scenarios to TWG's specific drivers, value chain, and geographies.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: In 2023, The Warehouse Group engaged with other New Zealand retailers that are climate reporting entities and KPMG New Zealand to develop shared scenarios for the New Zealand retail sector ("Retail Sector Scenarios"). These scenarios are detailed in a published report entitled "The Futures of Retail". [...] We would like to thank and acknowledge our New Zealand retail peers for their participation in the New Zealand retail sector's scenario-setting process in 2023 which formed the start of this journey.
answers.notes: The document describes collaborative development of the Retail Sector Scenarios with NZ retail peers and KPMG NZ. While the exact keywords "participatory", "co-creat", or "co-design" are not used, the description of joint development with multiple retailers and an external consultant constitutes co-production. The internal downscaling process also involved cross-functional workshops with internal stakeholders.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.evidence: The framework architectures of the Orderly, Disorderly and Hot House scenarios developed by the retail sector are based on an internally coherent set of socio-economic assumptions, decarbonisation pathways, and climate change projections. Following the NZ CS guidance, this architecture combines distinctive and diverse higher-level, publicly available scenarios and projections to provide broad guidelines.
answers.notes: The document does not describe a separate baseline or reference scenario against which the three climate scenarios are compared. The three scenarios (Orderly, Disorderly, Hot House) are presented as alternatives to each other, not compared against a baseline. The "Current Policies" (Hot House) scenario could be considered a de facto reference, but it is not explicitly labelled as a baseline.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: The process was a stand-alone analysis, no modelling was undertaken, and it was not integrated into our usual strategy processes.
answers.notes: The document explicitly states that no modelling was undertaken. No climate models, IAMs, or macroeconomic models are named anywhere in the document.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Freight Disruption: Disruption to supply chain freight in extreme weather events. Some Group shipments may not arrive in New Zealand for key trading periods, impacting revenue potential. [...] Trading Disruption: New Zealand experiences more frequent, severe, and less predictable weather systems, leading to some parts of the country being uneconomical to live in. In the short term this may impact our ability to trade and our logistics might not be able to access sites. In the long term some stores may become untradeable. [...] Factory Disruption: China and Bangladesh are expected to be hit harder than New Zealand by the physical impacts of climate change, making it increasingly difficult to source from these countries. This may lead to a loss and decrease in profit, increased cost of goods and increased supply chain costs.
answers.notes: The document identifies acute physical risks (extreme weather events, floods, disruptions) and describes their anticipated business impacts (revenue loss, supply chain disruption, increased costs). However, no quantified financial damage or loss estimates are provided — the Group has applied Adoption Provision 2 deferring anticipated financial impact quantification.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: Policy Reaction to Climate Change | Immediate and smooth | Delayed to fast uncoordinated change | Slow change. Technology Change | Fast change | Slow to fast change | Slow change. Consumer Sentiment | Rapid reorientation towards sustainable lifestyles [...] | Current trends continue to 2030, then abruptly transition [...] | Current consumption trends continue [...]. Carbon Taxes [...] Insurance Premiums [...] Climate Regulations [...] Trading Disruption: New Zealand experiences more frequent, severe, and less predictable weather systems [...] Availability of Resources: Volatility in the supply of raw materials caused by the impact of climate change.
answers.notes: These variables are used to characterise the scenarios and drive risk/opportunity identification, though no quantitative modelling of these variables was performed (the analysis was qualitative).

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: The Warehouse Group has downscaled the Retail Sector Scenarios and conducted qualitative analysis to develop its climate scenarios [...] These scenarios are consistent with the retail sector scenarios (but downscaled for TWG's own business) [...] We used publicly available Scenarios, including the Network for Greening Financial System (NGFS), Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), and the New Zealand Climate Change Commission (CCC) Scenarios.
answers.notes: The scenarios start from global frameworks (NGFS, IPCC SSPs) and have been downscaled to the NZ retail sector level and further to TWG's own business context (covering NZ, China, Bangladesh, Australia). The downscaling is described as qualitative rather than quantitative climate modelling.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short-term: 2024–2030; Medium-term: 2031–2040; Long-term: 2041–2050. Rationales are provided for each time horizon.
answers.evidence: Time horizons | Short-term: 2024-2030 | While the retail sector operates on a one to three year time horizon, 2024-2030 aligns with the New Zealand retail sector scenario setting, as well as the Group's own five-year strategic planning process. | Medium-term: 2031-2040 | We have aligned with the New Zealand retail sector scenario setting, and encompassed the 10-year period between short-term and allowing for a 10-year long-term period up to 2050. | Long-term: 2041-2050 | We have aligned with both international and New Zealand commitments to limit the global temperature increase to 1.5 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels, and global ambitions for net zero emissions to be attained by 2050.
answers.location_hint: Page 14, Scenario boundaries and time horizons table
answers.notes: Rationales are explicitly provided for each time horizon: short-term aligns with the NZ retail sector and the Group's 5-year planning; medium-term covers the 10-year gap; long-term aligns with 1.5°C and net zero 2050 commitments.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: The Group's first scenario, Orderly decarbonisation, and third scenario, Hot House, align with the mandated NZ CS scenarios. We selected our second scenario, Disorderly, to represent a challenging future for our business and industry as it is designed to explore a disrupted climate transition. These scenarios are consistent with the retail sector scenarios (but downscaled for TWG's own business), and therefore support comparability with the disclosures of other retailers. No further scenarios have been explored.
answers.notes: The justification covers: (1) regulatory compliance — Orderly and Hot House align with mandated NZ CS scenarios; (2) business relevance — Disorderly was chosen to explore a disrupted climate transition; (3) sector comparability — consistency with retail sector scenarios supports comparability with other retailers' disclosures.
